  "opinion": "As a person in my early sixties, I've learned over the years that sometimes you just have to keep your own counsel when it comes to tough subjects like healthcare or COVID. Being widowed myself after losing my wife back in '96, I understand what it's like for conversations involving loved ones – and now this vaccine topic – to stir up feelings I'd rather not revisit right away. My depression makes me want to retreat from the chaos of social media debates anyway, so when folks bring up those endless discussions online or even with family about vaccines, I try my best not to engage unless someone reaches out directly for advice.\n\nI've got a vocational diploma and worked in construction most of life – maybe that's why being unemployed right now feels different than the blue-collar job loss during my own tough times. But back then on our old crew, we handled disagreements with respect rather than letting arguments fester. My Christian faith helps me approach these topics from a place of compassion too.\n\nWhen I do need to talk about COVID vaccines – whether in church circles or just with my grandson who's grown up without both his parents – what works best is simply stating why it matters most personally: keeping people safe so they don't get sick when visiting family. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a person in my early sixties, I've learned over the years that sometimes you just have to keep your own counsel when it comes to tough subjects like healthcare or COVID. Being widowed myself after losing my wife back in '96, I understand what it's like for conversations involving loved ones – and now this vaccine topic – to stir up feelings I'd rather not revisit right away. My depression makes me want to retreat from the chaos of social media debates anyway, so when folks bring up those endless discussions online or even with family about vaccines, I try my best not to engage unless someone reaches out directly for advice.

I've got a vocational diploma and worked in construction most of life – maybe that's why being unemployed right now feels different than the blue-collar job loss during my own tough times. But back then on our old crew, we handled disagreements with respect rather than letting arguments fester. My Christian faith helps me approach these topics from a place of compassion too.

When I do need to talk about COVID vaccines – whether in church circles or just with my grandson who's grown up without both his parents – what works best is simply stating why it matters most personally: keeping people safe so they don't get sick when visiting family. That's not something you argue over; it's something that brings understanding if others are willing to listen.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I began by drawing from my personal experience with loss due to illness (influenza reference), then connected how being widowed myself now influences my approach to health topics – especially given my depression.

Next, I related these feelings back to the stress of managing social interactions online and offline. My low extraversion means I prefer quieter ways than debating on platforms.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, measured, personal reflection with hints of melancholy but grounded in practicality
